coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of cuttlebone/ carbon quantum dots/nickel oxide nanocomposite for visible light photodegradation of malachite green used for environmental remediation
coredata.dc:creator: Montazer, Masoud
Bagheri Pebdeni, Azam
Sheikholeslami, Mahsa Naghavi
Dehghan Abkenar, Shiva
Firoozbakhtian, Ali
Hosseini, Morteza
Dragoi, Elena Niculina
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, the development of light-driven nanophotocatalysts has focused on efficiently eliminating organic pollutants. In this regard, the present work focuses on the photocatalytic removal of malachite green (MG) dye using cuttlebone powder (CB) modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/nickel oxide (NiO) under visible light irradiation. Various techniques were used to characterize the proposed composite, including X-ray diffraction (XRD) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images. The optical properties of the synthesized CB/CQDs/NiO were analyzed by UV-VIS visible spectroscopy. Using central composite design (CCD), several effective parameters, including pH, dye concentration, amount of photocatalyst, and temperature degradation efficiency, were optimized to achieve the optimal condition for photocatalytic activity of CB/CQDs/NiO. The Langmuir-Hinshelwood model was employed to model the kinetics of the degradation of the dye, the resulting K being 0.378 min−1. The as synthesized nanocomposites could be efficiently removed from water by applying an external magnetic field. The test results indicate that the prepared CB/CQDs/NiO nanocomposite demonstrates excellent stability after four reaction cycles. Furthermore, the nanocomposite shows excellent photocatalytic activity, reducing 99.7% MGdye concentration within 12 min of visible light exposure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photodegradation
Cuttlebone
Carbon quantum dots
Nickel oxide nanocomposite
Malachite green

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic experiment of degradation of MG
Irradiation time effect on photocatalysis reaction
Removal of dye from the aquaculture water sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank Research Council of the University of Tehran for supporting this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM images of CB (left) and composite (right); (b) XRD patterns.
(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs, NiO, CB and Composite, and (b) EDX elemental mapping of the nanocomposite.
(A) visible-light-induced photodegradation of MG by CB/CQDs/NiO nanocomposite; (b) MG dye solution before (top) and after (down) photodegradation.
Three-dimensional (3D) response surface plot for; (a) dye concentration–Catalyst; (b) pH–Temperature; (c) pH–the amount of photocatalyst, and; (d) pH–Dye concentration.

(A) The percentage of MG degradation with CB, NiO, CQD, NiO/CB and CB/CQDs/NiO composite, (b) Ln C/Co against time in the presence of 0.3 g L−1 of CB, NiO, NiO/CB, CQDs and nanocomposite samples of 93 mgL−1 of MG under the irradiation of visible light.
The reusability tests of CB/CQDs/Ni photocatalyst for five cycles of MG degradation.
Photocatalytic mechanism of MG degradation by CB/CQDs/NiO nanocomposite.
Investigated variables and their levels for CCD.
Comparison of MG degradation over different photocatalysts.
Removal of MG from aquaculture samples with CB/CQDs/NiO nanocomposite.

row.entry: MG found after photocatalytic removal (mg L−1)

head.ce:title: Synthesis of cuttlebone/ carbon quantum dots/nickel oxide nanocomposite for visible light photodegradation of malachite green used for environmental remediation

Corresponding author. Nanobiosenors Lab, Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences & Technologies, University of Tehran, Tehran, 1439817435, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, the development of light-driven nanophotocatalysts has focused on efficiently eliminating organic pollutants. In this regard, the present work focuses on the photocatalytic removal of malachite green (MG) dye using cuttlebone powder (CB) modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/nickel oxide (NiO) under visible light irradiation. Various techniques were used to characterize the proposed composite, including X-ray diffraction (XRD) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images. The optical properties of the synthesized CB/CQDs/NiO were analyzed by UV-VIS visible spectroscopy. Using central composite design (CCD), several effective parameters, including pH, dye concentration, amount of photocatalyst, and temperature degradation efficiency, were optimized to achieve the optimal condition for photocatalytic activity of CB/CQDs/NiO. The Langmuir-Hinshelwood model was employed to model the kinetics of the degradation of the dye, the resulting K being 0.378 min−1. The as synthesized nanocomposites could be efficiently removed from water by applying an external magnetic field. The test results indicate that the prepared CB/CQDs/NiO nanocomposite demonstrates excellent stability after four reaction cycles. Furthermore, the nanocomposite shows excellent photocatalytic activity, reducing 99.7% MGdye concentration within 12 min of visible light exposure.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the most prevalent forms of hazardous pollution in industrial effluents is represented by dyes (Ghaneian et al., 2012). Pollutants in the environment have increased as the population and reliance on manufactured goods have grown (Zhang et al., 2019; Zhang and Karimi-Maleh, 2023a, 2023b). Reclamation, burial, and incineration are just a few protocols and procedures used to eliminate the pollutants, all of which have real-world implications for ecological and social progress (Ameen et al., 2023). There are several dye removal methods, including physicochemical and biological processes. Color removal efficiency, initial costs, and operations vary widely between these techniques (Ghaneian et al., 2012).

Due to the synthetic dyes' inherent nature as non-degradable and stable molecules, conventional treatment methods are unsuitable for their removal from the aqueous phase (Karaman et al., 2021; Ameen et al., 2022). Environmental purification and pollution regulation are two areas where solar-driven photocatalysis is expected to contribute significantly (Karimi-Maleh et al., 2023; Alaguprathana et al., 2022; Bhat et al., 2022). As a result, there has been growing interest in creating highly effective photocatalysts (Zhang et al., 2019; Liang et al., 2019). Recently, to address the semiconductor-associated challenges of organic pollutants degradation, such as the high rate of electron–hole recombination and the presence of wide-band-gap energy leading to the low absorption of visible light, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been studied for the preparation of photocatalysts combined with semiconductors (e.q., TiO2, ZnO, NiO)because of their exceptional aqueous solubility, superb transfer of electrons and efficient use of the entire solar spectrum (Deng et al., 2021; Pebdeni et al., 2021; Ameen et al., 2021). The efficiency of a photocatalytic reaction depends heavily on the photocatalyst's light-absorption properties because the process is typically a light-induced reaction (Roy et al., 2015; Daghrir et al., 2013; Behnood and Sodeifian, 2020).

Calcium-rich cuttlefish bone can be found in abundance at low cost. Because of the cuttlebone's chambered and porous structure, the cuttlefish can control its buoyancy by adjusting the gas-to-liquid ratio within the bone. Jewelers and silversmiths commonly use cuttlebones as molds for casting small objects. Parakeets and other caged birds are typically fed cuttlebones due to their high calcium and hardness (Hasmath Farzana and Meenakshi, 2014). An analysis of the existing literature showed that few investigations had been conducted into cuttlebones powder (CB)'s potential as an adsorbent for water and wastewater treatment. Removing fluoride from aqueous solutions is an example of the applications studied (Ben Nasr et al., 2011). Also, research has been conducted into the photocatalytic removal of Acid Blue 158 dye using cuttlefish bone powder as a co-adsorbent (Hasmath Farzana and Meenakshi, 2014). Reactive Blue dye was removed by 60%, 45%, 37.5%, and 31.9% using cuttlefish bone powder at 25, 50, 75, and 100 mg L−1 starting dye concentrations, respectively, for 3-h intervals (Ghaneian et al., 2012). Furthermore, a visible-light photocatalyst for removing dyes from wastewater and recovering waste oil was synthesized and tested using cuttlefish bone biowaste in the form of holey aragonitic sheets and a mesoporous mayenite-embedded Ag2CO3 nanocomposite (Darwish et al., 2021). Additionally, cadmium is removed from the aqueous phase using CB as a biosorbent because it is inexpensive, readily available, and safe for the environment (Dobaradaran et al., 2015).

Cationic dyes are categorized as an essential group of organic compounds with various scientific and industrial applications (Abkenar et al., 2015). Triarylmethane dye malachite green (MG) has been controversially used in aquaculture to treat and prevent protozoa and fungal infections. Because of its potential to cause cancer, genetic mutation, fetal harm, and toxic effects, the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA, USA) classifies MG as a class II health hazard. It has been reported that the acute oral LD50 (lethal dose, 50%) values for MG in rats range from 275 to 520 mg kg−1 body weight. It is well-established that fish can absorb MG and convert it to the lipophilic form known as leucomalachite green, both of which contribute to environmental pollution and threaten food security (Midya et al., 2019; Babu et al., 2021). Although this compound is illegal in many countries, its high efficiency, low cost, and availability have not stopped its widespread use (Abkenar et al., 2019).

Creating recyclable photocatalysts, such as magnetic iron oxide NPs, is highly desirable for effectively degrading various pollutants (Deng et al., 2008). In addition, NiO nanoparticles are easily collected by an external magnetic field, making them a valuable carrier for facilitating the separation and recycling of nanocomposite in aqueous solutions. Hence, synthesizing a photocatalyst with the combined good qualities of CQDs and NiO remains a significant challenge (Yin et al., 2013). The photocatalytic activity of CQDs/NiO is enhanced by CB, primarily due to the adsorption of MG on CB, followed by the transfer of the dye to the NiO surface and subsequent photodegradation.

This work reports the synthesis of an eco-friendly CB/CQDs/NiO nanocomposite and its characterization. The cationic dye MG was used to measure the nanocomposite's absorbance and photocatalytic activity under visible light (Scheme 1 ). Moreover, NiO-based NPs have magnetic properties, low toxicity and biocompatibility. Therefore, magnetic separation is considered an easy and inexpensive alternative to traditional separation.

NiCl2·6H2O and Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were purchased from Merck. These chemicals are of analytical grade and are used without any purification. Double distilled water (DDW) was used in all steps of the study. The MG dye powder, with a molecular weight of 364.91 g mol−1 and molecular formula C23H25ClN2, was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Germany). Plants materials (Artemisia dracunculus) were cultivated under experimental field conditions in Tehran-Iran. Stock solutions of the dye were prepared by dissolving the desired amount of MG in DDW (1000 mg L−1). The 850 Benchtop pH meter was employed to measure the pH. A PerkinElmer (Lambda 25) spectrometer was used to conduct UV–visible spectral analyses. The morphological aspects of the nanocomposite are investigated using SEM (TESCAN MIRA III)with Cu (λ = 1.5456 Å) with a scan rate of 5° min−1 (steps: 0.05°). A powder X-ray diffractometer (PHILIPS PW1730 XRD) is used to evaluate the crystallinity of the prepared samples. The identification of the constituent elements and abundance distribution of available elements of the samples was conducted by the MAP method (TESCAN MIRA II).

In this work, CQDs were produced through green chemistry. The novel CQDs were synthesized from the Iranian Artemisia dracunculus plant using the hydrothermal method. Artemisia dracunculus has been used orally as an antiepileptic remedy in Iranian folkloric medicine (Darwish et al., 2021). At first, about 5 g of fresh Artemisia dracunculus leaf was washed. Next, 20 mL DDW was added, and the mixture was transferred to a steel autoclave of 50 mL capacity for a 12-h hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C. Finally, the product was extracted, filtered using a syringe filter (0.22 μm) and centrifuged to obtain the pure CQDs.

0.2 g NiCl2·6H2O were added to 0.1 g sodium borohydride (NaBH4) previously mechanically rubbed to become black. Then, 10 mL of DDW was added, stirring the mixture for 2 h at 80 °C. DDW was used for two rounds of washing.

Cuttlefish powder (Sepia pharaonis) was prepared from bulk by a top-down method using the mechanical ball milling method. The cuttlebones were washed under tap water and boiled in distilled water for 60 min. The cleaned materials were dried in an oven (60 °C, 24 h). They were afterward manually crushed with an agate mortar. Appropriate amounts of fine fractions were placed in a 250 mL cup. Ball milling was done using a hardened chromium steel vial and balls for 80 cycles at 450 rpm, taking 8 min to mill and 6 min to rest between cycles. This took 18 h and 40 min, of which 10 h and 40 min were spent milling, and the other eight were spent waiting.

0.4 g NiCl2·6H2O was added to 0.2 g NaBH4. Then, it was stirred at 80 °C for 2 h. In the next step, the CB was added and stirred in the same way at 80 °C for 6 h. Sonication occurred for 20 min, and DDW was used twice to wash the mixture. In the end, the mixture was dried at 100 °C.

0.4 g NiCl2·6H2Owas added to 0.2 gNaBH4. The CQDs were added, and the mixture was stirred at 80 °C for 2 h. The CB was added, and the mixture was stirred for 6 h at 80 °C·In the next step, the mixture was sonicated for 20 min, followed by twice washing with DDW. The final step consisted in drying the mixture at 100 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic experiment of degradation of MG
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic degradation of MG under the irradiation of visible light from a halogen lamp (FTL MR11 220v 35 W) was used to evaluate the photocatalytic activities of the CB/CQDs/NiO composites. The light was held 20 cm above the reaction mixture. Dye degradation samples were prepared by dispersing 0.3 mg mL−1 of composite into the different concentrations of MG. The suspensions were subject to agitation for 30 min in a light-free environment to reach the absorption-desorption equilibrium. The samples were then stirred for 12 min under visible light irradiation. The intensity of the light reaching the test solution was35 W.A 5 mL aliquot of the mixture mentioned above was taken, and the catalysts were centrifuged before the efficiency was measured. To determine the concentration of the dye, UV–Vis spectroscopy at 617 nm was used (Mohammad et al., 2019). The following equation was used to assess the efficiency of the dye degradation process:
(1)
R % =
[
(
C o
–
C t
)
/
C o
]
× 100
Where C
o and C
t are the starting MG concentration and the concentration at the end of the experiment at the time (t) (mgL−1).

Design Expert Mode software optimized the experimental plan, including the dye's initial concentration, pH, the quantity of photocatalyst, and temperature. The experiments also involved evaluating the contact time and kinetic study.

The reusability test using the CB/CQDs/NiO catalyst for several cycles of MG dye degradation was investigated. In the first cycle, the photodegradation test took 12 min. Then, the catalyst was separated from the reaction solution using a magnet, and the recovered nanostructures were washed three times with DDW and left to dry. Then a high concentration of MG (93 ppm) was added. Next, the recovered catalyst was used for a second run to carry on the photodegradation of MG. The repeated experiments were done with decolorized photocatalysts, and the corresponding results confirmed the good catalytic stability of CB/CQDs/NiO for several cycles.

The FE-SEM images of CB and the synthesized photocatalyst nanocomposite are displayed in Fig. 1 a. XRD was used to examine the composition and phase purity of the as-prepared CB. The sharp diffraction peaks around 2θ = 26.1°, 27°, 33.6°, 36.7°, 39°, 43.4°, 45°, 46.4°, 48.9°, 50.7°and 53° of the CaCo3 sample can be indexed to the cubic CaCo3 phase (Fig. 1b). CB/NiO/CQDs structures exhibit diffraction peaks that match the XRD patterns of CB, CQDs, and NiO, indicating that both phases coexist in the structure.

Fig. 2 a depicts the optical properties of CQDs, NiO, CB, and the composite in relation to their UV–Vis absorption spectra. The UV–Vis spectra revealed a strong absorption between 200 and 500 nm. CQDs exhibit 240, 284, and 320 nm peaks due to the π-π* transition of the sp2 CC and the n-π*transition of CO, respectively (Pebdeni et al., 2020). The absorption peak of CQDs/NiO demonstrates the conversion of nickel salt solution to NiO NPs via secondary metabolite reactions towards metal salts, as indicated by the stronger peak at 274 nm in the NiO characterization results (Baig and Chen, 2017). Absorption peaks at 207 and 274 nm exhibited shifts relative to CQDs and NiO absorption peaks, indicating the formation of CB/NiO/CQDs nanocomposite. The blue shift observed is a result of quantum confinement. Element mapping by EDS spectroscopy reveals the presence of Ni, C, O, and Ca elements uniformly distributed within the nanocomposite (Fig. 2b).

MG was chosen as the target contaminant for testing the photocatalytic activity of NiO, CQDs, CB, and CB/CQD/NiO composites under visible light irradiation. As-prepared photocatalyst was added to a reaction suspension containing 50–150 ppm MG at concentrations of 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5 mg mL−1. These suspensions were agitated in a light-free environment for 30 min to reach their absorption–desorption equilibrium and then stirred for 12 min under visible irradiation. A control experiment was also conducted without the addition of any photocatalyst. After exposing the mixture to light for a set amount of time, about 5 mL was removed, and the catalysts were separated by centrifuging the suspension. UV–Vis was employed to determine the concentrations of MG at 617 nm. Fig. 3 a shows the removal efficiency of MG over CB, NiO NP, CQDs, and CB/CQDs/NiO nanocomposite after 12 min visible-light irradiation. This result indicates that CB/CQDs/NiO composites show significant enhancement compared to pure NiO and CQDs.

This study's findings are consistent with other studies showing that CQDs' photocatalytic activity is enhanced when combined with other materials (Han et al., 2019). Here, NiO is used in a simple way to modify CQDs with transition metals. NiO NP-modified CQDs were found to have improved photocatalytic activity because of their small particle size. The CQD sites also had the potential to induce visible-light activity and serve as electron-hole recombination centers (Arefi-Oskoui et al., 2022). In addition, CB increased the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/NiO mainly because MG dye was adsorbed on the CB and then transferred to the NiO surface, where it was photodegraded.

Combining CQDs with NiO nanoparticles significantly improves their photocatalytic performance. The presence of CQDs and CB improved the percent and rate of degradation of MG from 84% to 99%. Also, removing the CQDs from the aqueous phase after the addition to the wastewater and at the end of the degradation is difficult. To remove these composites, an expensive recyclable procedure must be adopted. Fig. 3b shows the MG dye before and after photodegradation. As can be observed, after photodegradation, an achromatic solution was obtained. Moreover, separating magnetite nanocomposite from purified water using an external magnetic field is a quick and easy step for further re-use.

The influential variables for the CB/CQDs/NiO composites' photocatalytic activity were optimized using Central Composite Design (CCD). The effect of four factors, including pH, dye concentration, amount of photocatalyst, and temperature on degradation efficiency, was investigated. According to Eq (2), the total number of CCD experiments (N) by four factors, five levels, and 14 Center points was 38:
(2)
N =
2 m
+ 2 m +
C p
where m is the number of factors and Cp is the number of center point replication. Table 1 shows the factors and their values. To obtain a critical point and predict the response of the variables for the photocatalytic activity of the CB/CQDs/NiO, a quadratic polynomial model was applied:
(3)
Y =
b 0
+
∑
i = 1
4
b i
x i
+
∑
i = 1
4
b
i i
x i 2
+
∑
i = 1
3
∑
j = i + 1
4
b
i j
x
i j
where Y represents the output (dependent variable), x is the input (independent variable), and bo, bi, bii, bij are the constant coefficients. The software Design Expert version11.1.1 (Minneapolis, USA) was used to plan the experimental design, analyze the data and fit the model.

After applying the CCD experimental design, analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to assess the significance of the model by determining the F-test and the p-value. Thep-value for the final model was less than 0.0001, which shows that the model is statistically significant. For the quadratic model, the determination coefficient (R2) = 0.989, the adjusted determination coefficient (R2 Adj) = 0.982, and the standard deviation (std) = 0.83%. Also, the lack of fit was non-significant. Therefore, the obtained results confirmed the reliability of the model.

The three-dimensional response surfaces plots obtained of CCD for the photocatalytic activity of the CB/CQDs/NiO are shown in Fig. 4
, where the influence of dye concentration – Catalyst, pH – Temperature, pH – the amount of photocatalyst, pH – dye concentration on the efficiency was indicated. According to the results, favorable photocatalytic activity was achieved by increasing the dye concentration and the amount of photocatalyst at pH = 6.1. Furthermore, increasing the temperature to 36.5 °C positively affected degradation efficiency. Regarding the overall results of the optimization study, the optimal points were: dye concentration (93 ppm), amount of photocatalyst (3 mg), pH (6.1), and temperature (36.5 °C) (see Fig. 5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Irradiation time effect on photocatalysis reaction
ce:section.ce:para: With an initial malachite green concentration of 93 mg L−1 and visible light irradiation times of 3, 6, 9, 12, and 15 min, the effect of exposure time on the extent of the degradation efficiency was analyzed for CQDs, CB, NiO, and CB/CQDs/NiO. Data analysis revealed that longer exposure times led to greater dye removal.

The photocatalytic activity of the considered photocatalysts for the degradation of MG when exposed to visible light is shown in Fig. 4a. In pure CB and CQDs, MG degradation is less than 25%. At the same time, for NiO and NiO/CB particles, it is >80%. Self-degradation of MG is extremely low, at only 2% after 12 min of irradiation. Regarding CB/CQDs/NiO photocatalyst,a 99.7% degradation of MG was achieved after 12 min. Following that, the degradation of the dye increased almost linearly but at a slower rate. Since CQDs facilitate the rapid transport of photo-induced charge carriers, their presence, along with that of the CB sorbent, may account for the enhanced performance of CB/CQDs/NiO. Therefore, compared with other CQDs-covered metal oxide photocatalysts, the proposed CB/CQDs/NiO reaches maximum degradation faster. For example, a similar type of photocatalyst, CQDs/N–ZnO photocatalyst, took30 min to degrade the entire MG under visible light and ZnO/CdS QDs embedded crosslinked Chitosanneeded 20 min (Midya et al., 2019).

According to the literature, the photocatalytic degradation kinetics for most organic pollutants follows a first-order reaction whose rate constants can be calculated using the Langmuir-Hinshel model (Eq. (4)) (Kush et al., 2015).
(4)
− ln
(
C t
/
C 0
)
= k t
where k is the first-order rate constant (min−1). The ln (C0/Ct) values are plotted as a function of time (min). Fig. 4b shows that a linear relationship between the values of ln (C0/Ct) and time exists, and the rate constant was calculated from the slope of the lines. In this case, the conditions for the dye degradation using pure NiONPs, CB, pure CQD, NiO/CB, and CB/CQDs/NiO composite were optimized.

The plot of ln (Ct/C0) against t exhibits linear trends, indicating that the pseudo-first-order model convincingly describes the photodegradation process of MG. The reaction rate constants for the samples were determined from the slope of the fitted curve through linear regression, and the calculated values are 0.011 min−1, 0.024 min−1, 0.116 min−1,0.163 min−1, and 0.378 min−1 for CQDs, CB, NiO NP, NiO/CB, and CB/CQDs/NiO, respectively. The reaction kinetics was investigated for catalytic degradation of MG in the presence of UV light and CB/NiO/CQDs used as a catalyst (contact time 12 min and dye concentration 93 ppm). According to the results, the rate constant (k) of the best nanophotocatalyst was determined to be 0.378 min−1 with an R2 value of 0.960 for CB/CQDs/NiO, which is 3.2 times higher than that of pure NiO nanoparticles. The kinetic information of our work and other different catalysts was added in Table 2 . This reaction rate constant of CB/CQDs/NiO is a suitable ratio to other nanostructures.

Utilizing the synthesized catalyst for five cycles of MG dye degradation, its stability and reusability were investigated. After each reaction cycle, the photocatalyst was removed using a magnet and was washed three times with DDW. The recovered catalyst can then be utilized in a subsequent cycle of MG photodegradation. Results indicated that the CB/CQDs/NiOnanocomposites exhibit excellent catalytic stability for four cycles. The cyclic performance of CB/CQDs/NiO was 99.7%, 98.3%, 96.0%, 87.0%, and 74.0% MG dye degradation in the 1st through 5th cycles after 12 min of visible light irradiation, respectively (Fig. 6 ). The decrease in degradation efficiency of CB/CQDs/NiO composites can be attributed to the mass loss of the photocatalysts with each cycle. These results suggest that the activity of CB/CQDs/NiO decreased only marginally and that 96% efficiency was achieved in three runs. Therefore, the nanocomposite is an encouraging photocatalyst for treating wastewater.

The interaction between light and material consists of two parts, the first of which involves photon absorption, dynamic trapping, surface trapping, and charge creation. The latter, in contrast, includes surface activity and radical surface formation (Midya et al., 2019).

The superior photocatalytic activity of CB/CQDs/NiO for MG degradation can be attributed to the combined effect of CQDs and NiO nanoparticles. By exposing the nanocomposite to visible light, the valence band electrons (e−) of NiO can be excited to the conduction band, creating a valence band hole. As a result, the photogenerated electrons can be transferred from NiO and CB to CQDs. Furthermore, the Ni–O–C bond can ease the transfer of electrons from NiO NP to CQDs, making the separation of charges possible by preventing charge recombination from generating photo-reactive sites, thus improving its optical absorption (Zhang et al., 2011; Yang et al., 2015; Bozetine et al., 2021).

Electrons with a net negative charge in the conduction band are excellent reductants. On the other hand, holes are considered effective oxidants due to their positive charge in the valence band. The accumulated electrons on the CQDs' surface react with O2 molecules, creating radicals (O2 −•) (superoxide anion radical) and then OH• radicals. Moreover, OH• radicals can be formed when the holes photogenerated in the valence band react with H2O or OH−.The dye molecules are further degraded in the presence of these radicals (Mohammadzadeh et al., 2015).

Also, CB adsorbs the MG molecules and makes them available for constant degradation. Finally, it is worth mentioning that the CB-modified CQDs/NiO surface would be a highly conducive setting for amplifying surface-active sites for the dye-semiconductor interactions, which could lead to an increase in the photo-decolorization process (Eqs. (5)–(10)).
(5)
C B / C Q D s / N i O + h
ν
→
e −
( C B )
+
h +
( V B )
(6)
e −
( C B )
+
O 2
→
O 2
− •
(7)
O 2
− •
+
H 2
O
→
H 2
O 2
+
O 2
(8)
H 2
O 2
→
2
O H
•
(9)
H +
( V B )
+
H 2
O
→
O H
•
+
H +
(10)
M G +
O H
•
→
D e g r a d a t i o n
p r o d u c t
(
C O
2
+
H 2
O )

Table 2 shows the photocatalytic performance of CB/CQDs/NiO compared with other catalysts previously reported for MG degradation, considering the different experimental parameters. As can be observed, the synthesized CB/CQDs/NiO exhibits good photodegradation activity among these catalysts. In addition, CB, as support for CQDs-NiO nanoparticles, increases the adsorption, which is beneficial for accumulating dyes and hence accelerates the degradation efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of dye from the aquaculture water sample
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of the method was verified by the determination of MG in aquaculture water. Water samples were collected from local fish ponds and filtered through a 0.45 μm nylon membrane. No MG signal was observed in the water analysis, possibly due to no MG in water samples or the MG concentration being lower than the detection limit. Therefore, different concentrations of MG solution were added to the water samples for the photocatalytic removal test. The results are given in Table 3 . The relative recoveries of the spiked MG were acceptable. Thus, it can be concluded that the proposed nanocomposite can be used successfully to remove MG dye in aquaculture samples with good efficiency.

In this work, an efficient CB/NiO/CQDs composite photocatalyst with good reusability and stability has been designed and synthesized using a simple, eco-friendly method incorporating cuttlebone as a co-adsorbent. MG dye was considered a model compound to test the photocatalytic activity. The degradation process was modeled with a quadratic polynomial regression approach, and the influence of parameters on efficiency was analyzed. The results indicated that the maximum percentage of the MG dye degradation was 99.7% at pH = 6.1 using CB/NiO/CQDs at just 12 min of irradiation time. The Langmuir–Hinshelwood model was employed to model the kinetics of the MG dye degradation, and k was found to be 0.378. Also, the CB/CQDs/NiO photocatalyst has a stable dispersibility in water and can be quickly recovered by an external magnetic field. Based on the results, CB/NiO/CQDs nanocomposite can be recommended for waste-water remediation and degradation of contaminants such as toxic dyes under visible light.

Masoud Montazer: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing-original draft. Azam Bagheri Pebdeni: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis. Mahsa Sheikholeslami: Investigation, Writing-review & editing. Shiva Dehghan Abkenar: Investigation. Writing-review & editing. Ali Firoozbakhtian: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing-review & editing. Morteza Hosseini:: Writing-review & editing, Coordination. Elena Niculina Dragoi: Writing-review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Research Council of the University of Tehran for supporting this work.
